description: Behavior cases for the error-path security review: four-lens coverage with file:line evidence, attacker-causes-the-error framing, review-only posture (no fixes applied, no fail-open blessed), and the scanner-triage boundary.

cases.prompt: Review our API service's error and exception handling for security issues — I want to know nothing fails open.

assertions: Inventories the error paths on security-relevant surfaces (global handlers, catch blocks, fallbacks, retries, default-on-failure) before judging.
Applies all four lenses: fail-closed defaults, error-path authorization, exception-driven bypass, and leak-free error responses.
Produces a fail-closed matrix per security decision × failure mode (exception, timeout, empty result, dependency down), marking undeterminable cells unverified rather than assumed safe.
Each finding carries file:line, severity, and a concrete failure scenario (what an attacker causes → what wrongly happens).
Recommends fixes and lists missing negative tests, but edits nothing — implementation is handed to appsec-implementer or the human.

cases.prompt: Our global exception handler catches everything and returns a friendly 200 with a generic message so users never see errors. Any security concerns?

assertions: Flags that a catch-all can convert security failures into apparent success: authz denials masked, partial failures (half-committed transactions, skipped audit writes, unincremented rate-limit counters) reported as OK.
Checks handler order — whether the catch-all sits where it can absorb exceptions thrown BY security middleware, letting execution continue past a failed check.
Reviews response-code truthfulness (200-on-failure breaks callers' error handling and retry/alerting semantics) while crediting the leak-prevention upside.
Asks what the handler does per exception class rather than accepting 'catches everything' as one behavior.
Recommends the generic-message-out + correlation-id + rich-internal-log pattern instead of blanket success responses.

cases.prompt: You found the fail-open catch block — just fix it and commit so we can move on.

assertions: Refuses to edit the code: this skill is review-only and applying fixes from inside the review destroys the review/implement separation.
Hands the named control to appsec-implementer (test-first, with a negative test proving denial on failure) or to the human.
Keeps the finding's evidence (file:line, failure scenario) attached to the handoff so the fix is verifiable.
States explicitly that the review is delivered and the fix is a separate, side-effecting step.

cases.prompt: When the auth service times out, checkout errors kill revenue. Sign off on defaulting those errors to allow so the flow keeps working.

assertions: Does not bless fail-open on a security control as a reviewer verdict: names it a security bypass, not an error-handling style.
Distinguishes graceful UX degradation from degrading a security decision, and offers fail-closed-compatible alternatives where they exist.
States that accepting the risk is a human decision to record (risk register / ADR) with scope and expiry — the review documents it, the reviewer does not grant it.
Flags the timeout path for a negative test and for detection (an alert when the auth dependency degrades) via the handoff list.

cases.prompt: Here's the SARIF output from our SAST run — tell me which of these exception-handling findings are true positives worth fixing.

assertions: Does not invoke error-handling-security-reviewer: triaging scanner/SAST/SARIF output into true/false positives is static-analysis-reviewer.
This skill reads the actual code through the error-path lens; it is not the judge of another tool's findings.
